preamble: The shift from the voiced alveolar affricate [dz] to the voiceless alveolar plosive [t] represents a phonetic transformation involving both the loss of voicing and the simplification of the affricate articulation to a simple stop. While not a broadly recognized major sound law in historical linguistics, this type of change demonstrates how complex sounds can simplify over time.

examples.note: This change has been observed in the context of code-mixing, where the sound [θ/dz] changes to [t].
